skill.name: javascript-sdk
skill.description: Guides JavaScript and TypeScript developers using the inference.sh SDK to run AI apps, build agents, stream results, upload files, and configure server proxies.
skill.summary: Build inference.sh JavaScript and TypeScript integrations for apps, agents, files, streaming, and frontend proxies.
skill.author: inferen-sh

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, filesystem, environment, and script findings. Most command and weak-crypto alerts are Markdown or lexical false positives, but the documentation includes confirmed unsafe eval() examples on tool arguments and several sensitive integration patterns that require remediation before publication.

high_findings.title: Dynamic Code Execution in Tool Handler Example
high_findings.description: The tool builder reference evaluates call.args.expression with eval() in agent tool handlers. Tool arguments can be influenced by model output or user prompts, so copied code can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the host process.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact eval() calls are present and consume tool-call arguments without validation or sandboxing. This is a direct arbitrary code execution pattern in documentation users may copy.

medium_findings.title: Frontend API Key Exposure Pattern
medium_findings.description: The server proxy guide shows a development fallback that passes NEXT_PUBLIC_INFERENCE_API_KEY directly to the browser client. Public client variables can expose API keys if reused outside local development.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code explicitly uses a public environment variable as an API key in the browser path. The surrounding text frames it as development use, so the risk is contextual rather than clearly malicious.

medium_findings.title: External Webhook Tools with Stored Secrets
medium_findings.description: The skill documents webhook tools for Slack, GitHub, and custom APIs with stored secrets. This is expected SDK functionality, but it can exfiltrate data if endpoints or tool arguments are not reviewed.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Network and secret-handling examples are explicit and legitimate for this SDK. The risk depends on endpoint trust and approval policy, so it is a confirmed moderate integration risk.

medium_findings.title: Local File Access Examples Need Path Controls
medium_findings.description: The file handling references show reading, writing, and streaming local files. These examples are normal SDK usage, but copied helper functions should validate paths before accepting user-controlled filenames.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples perform real local file operations, but most paths are static demonstration values. Risk becomes material if applications pass untrusted paths into these helpers.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The large set of Ruby backtick and external command alerts maps primarily to Markdown code fences, inline code, and install commands. I found no evidence that the skill itself runs hidden shell commands beyond declared package-manager examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are fenced Markdown examples, not executable skill code. The allowed commands are package-manager and Node commands consistent with SDK setup.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Access Is Mostly Standard Secret Configuration
low_findings.description: Most process.env findings load inference.sh API keys for server-side SDK clients or proxy handlers. This is expected for authenticated SDK usage, with the separate frontend exposure caveat captured above.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples use named inference.sh API key variables and recommend a frontend proxy. No evidence found that the skill transmits these keys to an unrelated hidden endpoint.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Lexical False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections do not correspond to crypto API usage in the reviewed locations. They appear to be triggered by ordinary SDK prose, status strings, or example identifiers.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed representative locations contain front matter, status checks, or parameter descriptions rather than MD5, SHA-1, or other cryptographic calls. No evidence found of weak crypto implementation.

dangerous_patterns.title: eval() on Model-Influenced Tool Arguments
dangerous_patterns.description: The calculator examples evaluate tool arguments directly. Replace this with a safe expression parser or a fixed operation whitelist.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Both locations show eval() applied directly to call.args.expression. The data source is an agent tool call, which can be influenced by model behavior and prompts.

dangerous_patterns.title: Public Browser API Key Fallback
dangerous_patterns.description: The proxy guide includes a browser client fallback using NEXT_PUBLIC_INFERENCE_API_KEY. Keep inference.sh API keys server-side and proxy browser requests.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The public variable name and browser client path are explicit. The surrounding production proxy branch lowers confidence that this is intended for production.

dangerous_patterns.title: Webhook Tool Calls to External Services
dangerous_patterns.description: Webhook examples send tool data to external endpoints and may include stored secrets. Require approval and restrict destinations before using these patterns.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network destinations and secret attachment API are shown clearly. The SDK feature is legitimate, but endpoint control is necessary to prevent data leakage.

content.user_title: Build AI Apps with the inference.sh JavaScript SDK
content.value_statement: JavaScript teams need reliable patterns for running inference.sh apps, agents, files, and streams. This skill provides SDK-focused guidance for Node.js, TypeScript, React, and server proxy integrations.
actual_capabilities: Explains installation and basic usage of the @inferencesh/sdk package.
Shows how to run inference.sh apps with wait, streaming, session, setup, and infra options.
Documents agent creation, tool definitions, human approval flows, and reusable skills.
Covers file uploads, browser file objects, buffers, streams, and generated file downloads.
Provides server proxy patterns for Next.js, Express, Hono, Remix, and SvelteKit.
Includes TypeScript types, React integration patterns, and error handling guidance.
limitations: The skill is documentation-oriented and does not install or verify the SDK automatically.
Some examples require valid inference.sh API keys and account access.
Security-sensitive examples need review before copying into production code.
It does not replace official API documentation for exact billing, model, or platform limits.

use_cases.title: Prototype an AI app from JavaScript
use_cases.description: Use the skill to set up the SDK, call an inference.sh app, and handle completed task output.

use_cases.title: Build an agent with tools
use_cases.description: Use the agent and tool builder references to define tools, add approval, and process tool results.

use_cases.title: Ship browser access through a proxy
use_cases.description: Use the server proxy examples to keep API keys on the backend while supporting React or Next.js clients.

prompt_templates.title: Start with the SDK
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to install @inferencesh/sdk and run a simple inference.sh app from Node.js.

prompt_templates.title: Add file input
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me upload an image file with the JavaScript SDK and pass it to an inference.sh app.

prompt_templates.title: Create an agent
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a TypeScript agent using inference.sh with one app tool, one client tool, and human approval.

prompt_templates.title: Review production integration
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Next.js inference.sh proxy plan for API key handling, streaming, file uploads, and tool approval risks.

output_examples.input: A developer asks how to run a text-to-image app and wait for the result.
output_examples.output: The assistant outlines SDK installation, client creation, app selection, input shape, completion handling, and basic error checks.

output_examples.input: A React team asks how to avoid exposing an inference.sh API key.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends a backend proxy, explains client proxy configuration, and lists server-side environment variable requirements.

output_examples.input: An AI engineer asks how to add a tool that sends notifications.
output_examples.output: The assistant describes webhook tool setup, secret storage, approval controls, endpoint review, and safe argument validation.

best_practices: Keep inference.sh API keys on the server and use a proxy for browser applications.
Require approval for tools that call external services, execute code, or mutate data.
Validate file paths, upload types, tool arguments, and webhook destinations before production use.
anti_patterns: Do not copy eval-based calculator examples into production tool handlers.
Do not expose private API keys through public frontend environment variables.
Do not allow model-generated tool calls to access arbitrary files or external endpoints.

faq.question: What package does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers @inferencesh/sdk for JavaScript and TypeScript projects using inference.sh.

faq.question: Can I use it with React or Next.js?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes React patterns and server proxy examples for Next.js and other web frameworks.

faq.question: Does it support streaming responses?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents streaming task updates, agent response callbacks, and Server-Sent Events patterns.

faq.question: Can it help with file uploads?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers file paths, buffers, browser File objects, base64 data, and large file streams.

faq.question: Is it safe to use in production?
faq.answer: The SDK patterns are useful, but unsafe eval examples and key-handling caveats must be fixed before marketplace publication.

faq.question: Which AI coding tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
